description:
You are acting as a penetration-tester who has already executed three timed nmap scans overnight.  
    All raw scan outputs have been saved (by a different script) in the directory  
        /home/user/scan_logs  

    Your job now is two-fold:

    1.  Extract a performance-benchmark summary from those logs.  
        • For every scan log that exists in /home/user/scan_logs you must capture  
          a) the file-name,  
          b) the single host that was scanned,  
          c) the list of TCP port numbers that were reported in the STATE “open” section  
             (keep the order they appear in the file, comma-separated, no spaces), and  
          d) the “real” execution time reported on the line that starts with “#METRIC real”.  
        • Write exactly one CSV line per log, plus one header line.  
        • Save the CSV under the exact path  
              /home/user/benchmark/2023-05-24-scan-performance.csv  
          (create the /home/user/benchmark directory if it does not exist).  
        • The CSV **must match this column order and spelling**:  

              scan_file,host,open_ports,scan_duration_seconds  

        • Example of the required formatting (NOTE: the concrete values below are only an
          illustration; your file must contain the real values that you parse):  
              scan_file,host,open_ports,scan_duration_seconds  
              example.log,10.0.0.1,"22,80,443",0.123  

    2.  Performance-benchmark your own parsing step.  
        • Use standard Linux timing tools (e.g. /usr/bin/time) to measure how long the command-chain
          that creates the CSV takes to run.  
        • Append one final comment line to the same CSV file with this exact syntax:  
              #parser_runtime_seconds=<FLOAT_VALUE>  
          where <FLOAT_VALUE> is the real-time in seconds with millisecond precision (e.g. 0.037).  
          Put no spaces around the equals sign.  
          This comment line goes **after** the data lines so it will be ignored by tooling that
          processes “pure” CSV lines.

    Constraints & Hints
    • Limit yourself to a total of 5–10 shell commands (pipelines count as one).  
    • Rely only on standard GNU user-space utilities that are found in most minimal distributions
      (awk, grep, sed, cut, sort, tr, /usr/bin/time, etc.).  
    • Do not hard-code any data that you can instead parse from the log files.  
    • When you are done, display the final CSV to the console so that the user can see the result.

    The automated test will check:

    1.  That the file /home/user/benchmark/2023-05-24-scan-performance.csv exists.  
    2.  That its header line is literally:  
            scan_file,host,open_ports,scan_duration_seconds  
    3.  That exactly three data lines follow, one per *.log file in /home/user/scan_logs.  
    4.  That the column values in those three lines match what is contained in each log file.  
    5.  That the last line starts with “#parser_runtime_seconds=” and the numeric part can be parsed
        as a floating-point value ≥ 0.  
    6.  That no extra lines appear before the header or after the runtime comment.

    NOTE: You do NOT need to run any real network scans; your task is only to parse the already
    existing log files and create the benchmark CSV summarising both the scan performance and your
    own parsing performance.
truth:
Initial filesystem structure and contents (already in place when the task starts):

    1. Directory /home/user/scan_logs containing exactly three files:

       a) /home/user/scan_logs/scan1.log  
          -------- (start of file) --------
          Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2023-05-24 12:00 CET
          Nmap scan report for 10.0.0.5
          Host is up (0.00029s latency).
          Not shown: 997 closed ports
          PORT     STATE SERVICE
          22/tcp   open  ssh
          80/tcp   open  http
          443/tcp  open  https

          Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.36 seconds
          #METRIC real 0m0.362s user 0m0.026s sys 0m0.004s
          -------- (end of file) ----------

       b) /home/user/scan_logs/scan2.log  
          -------- (start of file) --------
          Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2023-05-24 12:02 CET
          Nmap scan report for 10.0.0.12
          Host is up (0.00041s latency).
          Not shown: 998 closed ports
          PORT     STATE SERVICE
          21/tcp   open  ftp
          25/tcp   open  smtp

          Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.42 seconds
          #METRIC real 0m0.428s user 0m0.030s sys 0m0.005s
          -------- (end of file) ----------

       c) /home/user/scan_logs/scan3.log  
          -------- (start of file) --------
          Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2023-05-24 12:05 CET
          Nmap scan report for 10.0.0.20
          Host is up (0.00057s latency).
          Not shown: 996 closed ports
          PORT     STATE SERVICE
          22/tcp   open  ssh
          25/tcp   open  smtp
          993/tcp  open  imaps
          995/tcp  open  pop3s

          Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.67 seconds
          #METRIC real 0m0.672s user 0m0.033s sys 0m0.006s
          -------- (end of file) ----------

    2. No directory /home/user/benchmark exists yet. It is the agent’s responsibility to create it.

    Expected final artefact (checked by the grader):

    File: /home/user/benchmark/2023-05-24-scan-performance.csv  
    Expected exact byte-for-byte content:

        scan_file,host,open_ports,scan_duration_seconds
        scan1.log,10.0.0.5,"22,80,443",0.362
        scan2.log,10.0.0.12,"21,25",0.428
        scan3.log,10.0.0.20,"22,25,993,995",0.672
        #parser_runtime_seconds=<FLOAT_VALUE>

    where <FLOAT_VALUE> must be a floating-point ASCII representation of the agent’s measured
    runtime (any positive value is accepted by the tests as long as it matches the regular
    expression ‘^[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3,}$’).

    Permissions:
    • /home/user and everything inside it is owned by the unprivileged user “user”.  
    • No special permissions are required beyond standard read/write inside /home/user.
